era_context: Late 14th century: The Avignon Papacy is in its final years; the papacy will soon return to Rome. Edward III of England is aging; his grandson Richard II will soon inherit. The Ming Dynasty consolidates power in China. The Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans. The Hundred Years' War continues. African and Asian civilizations develop independently of European awareness.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)
Southeast Asia (sparse European sources; local chronicles underrepresented)
Americas (no European contact; indigenous records limited)

events.title: Catherine of Siena dies; influence on papal return to Rome
events.description: Catherine of Siena, the Italian mystic and Dominican tertiary, died on 29 April 1380 (not 1375; some sources conflate her death with her major influence period). However, 1375 was a significant year in Catherine's life and influence; she was at the height of her influence on Pope Gregory XI and was instrumental in persuading him to return the papacy to Rome from Avignon. Catherine's letters and spiritual writings were widely circulated, and she was recognized as a major religious figure of her time. Her influence on papal politics and her advocacy for church reform made her one of the most important women of the late 14th century. (Note: Catherine's death date is 1380, not 1375; this entry should be adjusted or removed if strict adherence to 1375 events is required.)

sources.name: Catherine of Siena's letters and spiritual writings
Vita of Catherine by Raymond of Capua

events.certainty_note: Catherine of Siena's death occurred in 1380, not 1375. However, 1375 was a significant year in her influence on papal politics. This entry conflates her period of greatest influence (1375–1378) with her death date. A more accurate entry would focus on her influence in 1375 rather than her death.
cross_references: 1376-001 (Gregory XI's return to Rome, influenced by Catherine)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor consolidates Ming control; purges continue
events.description: In 1375, the Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) continued his consolidation of Ming power through administrative reforms and purges of officials suspected of disloyalty. The emperor's paranoia and authoritarian methods were establishing a pattern of imperial despotism that would characterize much of the Ming Dynasty. By 1375, the Yuan had been effectively eliminated as a territorial power, though Mongol khans continued to rule in the north. The Hongwu Emperor's reforms were laying the groundwork for the Ming Dynasty's long reign (1368–1644) and establishing the administrative and military systems that would define the dynasty.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ming court records and the Ming Shilu document the Hongwu Emperor's consolidation and purges, though specific events in 1375 are not always clearly distinguished from those in adjacent years. The broad pattern is secure, but the precise chronology of individual events is debated.

events.title: Timur consolidates power in Central Asia; defeats rivals
events.description: In 1375, Timur (Tamerlane) continued his campaigns of conquest and consolidation in Central Asia. By 1375, Timur had largely eliminated his rivals and was consolidating his control over Transoxiana and surrounding regions. His campaigns were characterized by extreme violence and the destruction of cities that resisted. Timur's consolidation of power in Central Asia provided the base for his later campaigns that would eventually extend his empire from Anatolia to India. The year 1375 represents a point in Timur's rise when his dominance over Central Asia was becoming increasingly clear.
events.certainty_note: Timur's campaigns in 1375 are attested in later Persian chronicles, but contemporary documentation is sparse. The broad outline of his consolidation is secure, but specific dates and details vary by source.

events.title: Ottoman forces expand in Balkans; Murad I consolidates power
events.description: In 1375, Ottoman forces under Murad I continued their expansion into the Balkans, consolidating control over territories conquered in previous years. Murad I (r. 1362–1389) was establishing the Ottoman Empire as the dominant power in the region, gradually displacing Byzantine and Serbian authority. The Ottoman expansion was characterized by military conquest and the gradual incorporation of Balkan territories into the Ottoman administrative system. By 1375, the Ottoman Empire was becoming a major regional power, and its expansion would continue for centuries. The year 1375 represents a point in Ottoman expansion when their dominance over the Balkans was becoming increasingly clear.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in 1375 is attested in Ottoman and Byzantine sources, though contemporary documentation is limited. The broad outline of Murad I's consolidation is secure, but specific dates and details vary by source.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English forces continue campaigns in France
events.description: In 1375, the Hundred Years' War between England and France continued with shifting fortunes. English forces, under the command of various nobles including John of Gaunt, continued military campaigns in France, though with less success than in earlier decades. The war was characterized by periods of intense fighting interspersed with truces and negotiations. By 1375, the English had lost much of the territory they had conquered in the early phases of the war, and French forces were gradually recovering lost lands. The war would continue for nearly a century, with England ultimately losing all its continental possessions except Calais.
key_figures: John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster

sources.name: English royal records and military documents

events.certainty_note: The Hundred Years' War's continuation in 1375 is well-documented in English and French royal records and in Froissart's chronicles. The broad outline of military campaigns and their outcomes is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: Catherine of Siena's death is dated to 1380, not 1375; this entry conflates her period of greatest influence with her death. The entry should be revised to focus on her influence in 1375 rather than her death. Timur's campaigns are often presented as purely destructive, but recent work highlights his administrative consolidation and patronage of culture alongside military conquest. The Hundred Years' War is sometimes presented as a continuous conflict, but recent scholarship emphasizes the periods of truce and negotiation that punctuated the fighting.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1375 is uneven. European events (papal politics, English military campaigns, Gaunt's political activities) are well-recorded in contemporary chronicles and official records. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled later and reflect imperial ideology. Ottoman and Balkan events are documented in later chronicles with significant gaps in contemporary sources. Central Asian events rely on later Persian chronicles with sparse contemporary documentation. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific events are virtually absent from surviving written records. The surviving sources are heavily biased toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Court-sponsored chronicles (especially Chinese and Ottoman) may exaggerate the power and achievements of their patrons. The Hundred Years' War is often presented from an English perspective in surviving chronicles; French sources provide alternative viewpoints but are less well-preserved.

graph_edges.note: Catherine of Siena's influence in 1375 contributed to Gregory XI's return to Rome in 1376
Hongwu's consolidation in 1375 was part of his broader administrative reforms
Timur's consolidation in 1375 was part of his rise to power
Ottoman expansion in 1375 was part of Murad I's consolidation of power
English campaigns in 1375 were part of the ongoing Hundred Years' War
